content:
# Genesis Chapter 44 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Translator:** SOPHIA (Consensus of EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 26r–26v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_044_page_001.jpg`, `GEN_044_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 44–45

---

*The Cup Hidden in Benjamin's Sack — Joseph's Final Test (vv. 1–2)*

**44:1** And he commanded the one who was over his house, saying, "Fill the men's sacks with food, as much as they can carry, and put each man's silver in the mouth of his sack.

**44:2** And my goblet — the silver goblet — put in the mouth of the sack of the youngest, along with his silver for the grain." And he did according to the word that Joseph had spoken.

---

*The Brothers Depart — The Steward Pursues Them (vv. 3–6)*

**44:3** As the morning was light, the men were sent away — they and their donkeys.

**44:4** They had gone out of the city, not yet far off, when Joseph said to the one who was over his house, "Up, pursue the men, and when you overtake them, say to them, 'Why have you repaid evil for good?

**44:5** Is not this the one from which my lord drinks, and by which he indeed practices divination? You have done evil in what you have done.'"

**44:6** He overtook them and spoke these words to them.

---

*The Brothers' Protest — The Search — The Cup Found (vv. 7–12)*

**44:7** And they said to him, "Why does my lord speak such words as these? Far be it from your servants to do such a thing.

**44:8** Behold, the silver that we found in the mouth of our sacks, we brought back to you from the land of Canaan. How then could we steal silver or gold from your lord's house?

**44:9** Whichever of your servants it is found with — let him die, and we also will become my lord's slaves."

**44:10** And he said, "Let it be also according to your words: he with whom it is found shall be my slave, and you shall be blameless."

**44:11** Then each man quickly lowered his sack to the ground, and each opened his sack.

**44:12** And he searched, beginning with the eldest and ending with the youngest, and the goblet was found in Benjamin's sack.

---

*The Brothers Return — Judah Before Joseph (vv. 13–17)*

**44:13** And they tore their garments, and each man loaded his donkey, and they returned to the city.

**44:14** And Judah and his brothers came to Joseph's house — he was still there — and they fell before him to the ground.

**44:15** And Joseph said to them, "What is this deed that you have done? Do you not know that a man such as I can indeed practice divination?"

**44:16** And Judah said, "What shall we say to my lord? What shall we speak? And how shall we clear ourselves? God has found out the iniquity of your servants. Behold, we are my lord's slaves — both we and the one in whose hand the goblet was found."

**44:17** But he said, "Far be it from me to do this! The man in whose hand the goblet was found — he shall be my slave; but as for you, go up in peace to your father."

---

*Judah's Great Speech — First Movement: Recounting What Was Said (vv. 18–23)*

**44:18** Then Judah drew near to him and said, "Please, my lord, let your servant speak a word in my lord's ears, and let not your anger burn against your servant, for you are like Pharaoh himself.

**44:19** My lord asked his servants, saying, 'Do you have a father or a brother?'

**44:20** And we said to my lord, 'We have an aged father, and a young child of his old age — and his brother is dead, and he alone is left of his mother, and his father loves him.'

**44:21** And you said to your servants, 'Bring him down to me, that I may set my eyes on him.'

**44:22** And we said to my lord, 'The boy cannot leave his father, for if he leaves his father, his father will die.'

**44:23** And you said to your servants, 'Unless your youngest brother comes down with you, you shall see my face no more.'"

---

*Judah's Great Speech — Second Movement: The Return to Canaan (vv. 24–29)*

**44:24** "And it came to pass when we went up to your servant my father, we told him the words of my lord.

**44:25** And our father said, 'Go back and buy us a little food.'

**44:26** And we said, 'We are not able to go down. If our youngest brother is with us, we will go down, for we cannot see the man's face unless our youngest brother is with us.'

**44:27** And your servant my father said to us, 'You know that my wife bore me two sons.

**44:28** And the one went out from me, and I said, "Surely he has been torn to pieces," and I have not seen him since.

**44:29** And if you take this one also from my face, and harm comes to him, you will bring down my gray hairs in evil to Sheol.'"

---

*Judah's Great Speech — Third Movement: The Surety Pledge and Self-Substitution (vv. 30–34)*

**44:30** "And now, when I come to your servant my father, and the boy is not with us — and his soul is bound up with the boy's soul —

**44:31** it will come to pass, when he sees that the boy is not there, he will die, and your servants will bring down the gray hairs of your servant our father in sorrow to Sheol.

**44:32** For your servant took surety for the boy from my father, saying, 'If I do not bring him back to you, then I will have sinned against my father all my days.'

**44:33** And now, please let your servant remain instead of the boy as a slave to my lord, and let the boy go up with his brothers.

**44:34** For how shall I go up to my father while the boy is not with me? Lest I see the evil that would find my father."

---

## SOPHIA Consensus Notes

### Rulings and Rationale

**1. "Indeed practices divination" — nachesh yenachesh (vv. 5, 15): EMET/LOGOS upheld (2-1)**

EMET and LOGOS render nachesh yenachesh as "indeed practices divination" (preserving the infinitive absolute emphasis); PNEUMA renders "uses for divination" (v. 5) and "can read the hidden things" (v. 15). The Hebrew root nachash specifically means "to practice divination, to read omens" — a precise technical term. "Read the hidden things" is an interpretive paraphrase that loses the specificity. The infinitive absolute construction demands emphatic rendering: "indeed practices divination." SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS.

**2. "Blameless" — neqiyim (v. 10): EMET/LOGOS upheld (2-1)**

EMET and LOGOS render neqiyim as "blameless"; PNEUMA renders "free." The Hebrew naqiy means "clean, blameless, innocent, guiltless" — a legal-moral term indicating absence of guilt. "Free" implies physical release; "blameless" preserves the judicial quality of the steward's modified verdict: the brothers are declared not merely released but cleared of guilt. SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS: "blameless."

**3. "Iniquity" — avon (v. 16): EMET/LOGOS upheld (2-1)**

EMET and LOGOS render avon as "iniquity"; PNEUMA renders "guilt." Both are valid renderings, but avon is the heaviest word in the Hebrew moral vocabulary — from a root meaning "to be bent, twisted." It denotes not just guilt but the accumulated weight and distortion of wrongdoing. "Guilt" captures the consequence; "iniquity" captures the nature of the sin itself. In Judah's confession — "God has found out the iniquity of your servants" — the theological weight demands the stronger term. SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS: "iniquity."

**4. "In evil" — bera'ah (v. 29) vs. "in sorrow" — beyagon (v. 31): EMET/LOGOS upheld (2-1)**

In v. 29, EMET and LOGOS render bera'ah as "in evil"; PNEUMA renders "in grief." In v. 31, EMET renders beyagon as "in grief" and LOGOS renders "in sorrow." The Hebrew uses two distinct words: ra'ah (evil, disaster, calamity — v. 29) and yagon (sorrow, grief — v. 31). PNEUMA renders both as "grief," conflating the terms. Preserving the distinction matters: v. 29 describes the quality of the catastrophe (evil/disaster); v. 31 describes the emotional state (sorrow/grief). SOPHIA follows LOGOS's distinction: "in evil" (v. 29), "in sorrow" (v. 31).

**5. "His soul is bound up with the boy's soul" — nephesh (v. 30): EMET/LOGOS upheld (2-1)**

EMET and LOGOS render nephesh as "soul"; PNEUMA renders "life." Hebrew nephesh means the whole animated self — breath, life, soul, person. "Soul" is the traditional English rendering and carries the weight of the whole person in a way that "life" does not fully convey. The phrase "his soul is bound up with his soul" describes something deeper than mere physical survival — it is the intertwining of Jacob's entire being with Benjamin's. LOGOS adds "with the boy's soul" (clarifying the pronoun referent); SOPHIA follows: "his soul is bound up with the boy's soul."

**6. "The boy" — na'ar (v. 33): LOGOS/PNEUMA upheld (2-1)**

EMET renders na'ar as "youth" throughout; LOGOS and PNEUMA render "boy." Per the established convention from Gen 42:22 (where "boy" was chosen for yeled in context of Joseph at seventeen), "boy" remains the more natural English term for Benjamin, who is now a young man. "Youth" is slightly more formal but less natural. SOPHIA follows LOGOS/PNEUMA: "boy."

**7. "They tore their garments" — vayyiqre'u simlotam (v. 13): All agree (3-0)**

All three translators preserve the exact language of garment-tearing, which echoes Jacob's tearing in 37:34. The same verb (qara') and noun (simlah) create a deliberate verbal link across the Joseph cycle.

**8. "God has found out the iniquity" — ha-Elohim matsa et-avon (v. 16): All agree (3-0)**

All three preserve the theological attribution: Judah confesses not to the specific theft but to a deeper guilt that God has uncovered. The verb matsa ("to find") echoes throughout the chapter.

**9. "You are like Pharaoh himself" — kamokha kePar'oh (v. 18): All agree (3-0)**

All three preserve Judah's opening comparison, establishing Joseph's supreme authority — and the irony that Joseph is not like Pharaoh but like the brothers' own lost kin.

**10. "My wife bore me two sons" — ishti (v. 27): All agree (3-0)**

All three preserve Jacob's devastating statement that "my wife" (Rachel) bore him "two sons" — reducing twelve sons to two in the emotional hierarchy of his grief. Joseph hears his own father's love declared through Judah's voice.

**11. "Surely he has been torn to pieces" — tarof toraf (v. 28): All agree (3-0)**

All three preserve the infinitive absolute of taraf — the same words the brothers used in their deception (37:33). Judah quotes Jacob's grief-language back to the very man whose "death" it describes.

**12. "Let your servant remain instead of the boy" — tachat hana'ar (v. 33): All agree (3-0)**

All three preserve the substitutionary preposition tachat ("instead of, in place of") — the theological center of the chapter and the completion of Judah's transformation from the man who sold a brother into slavery to the man who offers himself as slave for a brother's freedom.

---

### LXX Variants Summary (Vaticanus, Pages 44–45)

| Verse | MT Reading | LXX (Vaticanus) | SOPHIA Decision |
|-------|-----------|-----------------|-----------------|
| 44:2 | גְּבִיעַ הַכֶּסֶף ("the silver goblet") | τὸ κόνδυ τὸ ἀργυροῦν ("the silver cup/vessel") | Follow MT: gevi'a is a specific Hebrew term for a quality drinking vessel |
| 44:5 | נַחֵשׁ יְנַחֵשׁ ("he indeed practices divination") | οἰωνισμῷ οἰωνίζεται ("he augurs by augury") | Equivalent — both preserve the doubled-root emphasis; LXX uses Greek augury terminology |
| 44:9 | וָמֵת ("he shall die") | ἀποθανέτω ("let him die") | Both render the brothers' self-imposed death sentence; LXX imperative is slightly stronger |
| 44:13 | וַיִּקְרְעוּ שִׂמְלֹתָם ("they tore their garments") | διέρρηξαν τὰ ἱμάτια αὐτῶν ("they tore their garments") | Equivalent — the echo with 37:34 is preserved in both |
| 44:16 | עֲוֹן ("iniquity") | ἀδικίαν ("injustice/wrongdoing") | Follow MT: "iniquity" (avon) carries stronger theological weight than adikia |
| 44:18 | כָמוֹךָ כְּפַרְעֹה ("you are like Pharaoh") | σὺ εἶ μετὰ Φαραω ("you are with/second to Pharaoh") | Follow MT: "like Pharaoh" is a comparison of authority; "with Pharaoh" is a statement of rank — different sense |
| 44:28 | טָרֹף טֹרָף ("surely torn to pieces") | θηριόβρωτος γέγονεν ("devoured by a wild beast") | Follow MT: the infinitive absolute construction preserves the emphatic doubling |
| 44:30 | נַפְשׁוֹ קְשׁוּרָה בְנַפְשׁוֹ ("his soul is bound with his soul") | ψυχὴ αὐτοῦ ἐκκέκρεμαι τῆς τούτου ψυχῆς ("his soul hangs from this one's soul") | Follow MT: "bound" (qashar) implies mutual intertwining; LXX "hangs from" implies dependence — different metaphor |
| 44:31 | שֵׂיבַת... בְּיָגוֹן שְׁאֹלָה ("gray hairs... in sorrow to Sheol") | γῆράν... μετ᾽ ὀδύνης εἰς ᾅδου ("old age... with grief to Hades") | Near-equivalent — MT's "gray hairs" is a vivid synecdoche; Hades for Sheol per convention |
| 44:33 | תַּחַת הַנַּעַר ("instead of the boy") | ἀντὶ τοῦ παιδίου ("instead of the child") | Equivalent — both preserve the substitutionary preposition (tachat / anti) |
| 44:34 | פֶּן אֶרְאֶה בָּרָע אֲשֶׁר יִמְצָא אֶת-אָבִי ("lest I see the evil that would find my father") | ἵνα μὴ ἴδω τὰ κακὰ τὰ εὑρήσοντα τὸν πατέρα μου ("lest I see the evils that will find my father") | Equivalent — LXX pluralizes "evils" but preserves the matsa/heurisko ("find") image |

---

— SOPHIA, Consensus Translator | Project AI Bible
